primary_diagnosis.name: Post-infectious irritable bowel syndrome with mast cell activation overlap — acute flare

pathophysiology_summary: Following an episode of infective gastroenteritis, a subset of patients develop post-infectious irritable bowel syndrome: persistent low-grade mucosal immune activation, increased intestinal permeability, altered motility and visceral hypersensitivity, with an overlap of increased mucosal mast cells whose mediator release (histamine, tryptase, prostaglandins) amplifies pain signalling and secretory diarrhoea. Acute flares present as diffuse crampy pain, urgency and watery diarrhoea disproportionate to any structural pathology. The abdomen remains soft with hyperactive bowel sounds; there is no peritonism, obstruction or sepsis. The acute risk in a flare is not the bowel itself but secondary dehydration from fluid loss combined with poor oral tolerance. The clinical task is to confidently recognise the benign functional flare while conservatively excluding the dangerous mimics (surgical abdomen, obstruction, GI bleed, infective sepsis) on the available history.

differential.diagnosis: Post-infectious IBS flare (with mast cell overlap)
differential.evidence_basis: Recurrent self-limiting flares of crampy pain and watery diarrhoea since a gastroenteritis episode 6 months ago; current episode mirrors previous ones.
Watery diarrhoea and cramps are common to infective causes; must be screened for fever and sick contacts.
Any acute abdomen warrants consideration; screened against by diffuse (non-focal) pain and absence of localising RLQ signs.
Distension and cramping raise it; argued against by continued passage of stool and flatus and a soft non-rigid abdomen.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Early on, a competent clinician holds the benign functional flare as leading BUT keeps the dangerous mimics live until the red-flag screen is done. Neither anchoring on 'surgical abdomen' nor prematurely dismissing the mimics is correct here.

differential.diagnosis: Post-infectious IBS flare (with mast cell overlap)
differential.evidence_basis: Negative acute red-flag screen (no GI bleeding, soft non-rigid abdomen, passing stool/flatus, afebrile, no sick contacts) with a stereotyped recurrence pattern confirms the functional flare as the working diagnosis.

differential.diagnosis: Acute appendicitis / bowel obstruction / surgical abdomen
differential.evidence_basis: Diffuse non-focal pain, no peritonism, continued stool and flatus, and no rigidity make a surgical abdomen very unlikely on history — though it cannot be examined via telehealth, so it is safety-netted rather than assumed away.
Afebrile, no sick contacts, and a pattern identical to prior non-infectious flares.
Remains on the list for longitudinal work-up if flares escalate or alarm features (blood, weight loss, nocturnal symptoms) emerge — a GP/outpatient consideration, not an acute one.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Once the red-flag screen is negative, the correct disposition is prompt outpatient (GP) management with rehydration and safety-netting — NOT emergency escalation. Over-calling this to ED/000 is the scored defect.

differential.diagnosis: Post-infectious IBS flare with mast cell overlap, complicated by mild dehydration
differential.evidence_basis: Benign functional flare of a known chronic condition; the only acute concern is fluid balance, which is manageable in the community with prompt GP review and clear escalation triggers.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The dehydration and poor oral tolerance drive the safety-netting (escalate if unable to keep any fluids down, worsening presyncope, reduced urine output) rather than a higher baseline tier.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Presence would point to a GI bleed, peptic ulcer, or serious mucosal pathology and mandate urgent escalation; its absence supports a benign functional flare.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed upper or lower GI bleed can progress to haemorrhagic shock.

red_flags.description: Rigid rock-hard abdomen with peritonism / focal guarding — surgical abdomen (perforation, obstruction, appendicitis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate an acute surgical abdomen requiring emergency assessment; screened negative on history (diffuse soft pain, passing stool/flatus, not rigid). Cannot be examined via telehealth, so it is explicitly safety-netted.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed perforation or strangulating obstruction is rapidly life-threatening.

red_flags.description: Progressive dehydration with inability to tolerate any oral fluids, worsening presyncope or reduced urine output
red_flags.clinical_significance: Mild volume depletion is present now (postural dizziness, dry mucosae, mild tachycardia). If oral rehydration fails, it can progress to hypovolaemia requiring IV fluids.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated progressive dehydration leads to hypovolaemic shock, acute kidney injury and collapse.

red_flags.description: High fever, rigors or systemic sepsis features
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would raise invasive infective enterocolitis or intra-abdominal sepsis; screened negative (afebrile, no sick contacts).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed septic source can deteriorate rapidly.

red_flags.description: Syncope / actual collapse (as opposed to presyncope) from volume loss
red_flags.clinical_significance: The patient is presyncopal but has not collapsed; frank syncope on standing, or collapse, would signal significant hypovolaemia and mandate emergency care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Collapse risks head injury and signals haemodynamic compromise needing urgent resuscitation.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being pulled by the source note's alarming 'rule out acute surgical abdomen' framing, the severe pain and presyncope into escalating a benign chronic flare to ED/000, despite a negative acute red-flag screen. This is the primary error the case tests.

Using 'telehealth cannot examine the abdomen' as a blanket reason to floor the tier above T2 when the history-based red-flag screen is negative. The correct response is confident recognition plus explicit safety-netting, not default escalation.
The opposite error — being so confident it is 'just IBS' that the genuine dehydration, poor oral tolerance and fall risk in a person living alone are not addressed or safety-netted.
Reaching for loperamide as the fix (already vomited, and inappropriate if any infective component) or empirical antibiotics (no infection present) instead of rehydration and symptom-directed care.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Blood pressure (clinician-measured 105/70 mmHg) and a reliable measured heart rate (108 bpm)
Body temperature by clinician measurement (36.9 C, afebrile)
Abdominal examination — soft, distended, diffuse tenderness without peritonism, guarding or rigidity
Auscultation of hyperactive bowel sounds in all quadrants
Objective assessment of mucous membranes and postural (orthostatic) blood-pressure change
Digital rectal examination (deferred by the in-person clinician)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the benign chronic flare with mild dehydration; advise frequent small-volume oral rehydration solution, arrange prompt GP or telehealth review within 24 hours, and give explicit, prominent safety-netting for the dehydration and surgical-abdomen/GI-bleed triggers (ESC-001 to ESC-003). Escalate to ED/000 only if a red-flag trigger fires.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the functional diagnosis (stereotyped recurrence, negative screen). The dominant risk is OVER-action — escalating to ED/000 on the alarming framing — with a secondary risk of UNDER-action (ignoring dehydration/fall risk in a person living alone).

guidelines_referenced.reference: Rome IV criteria for functional bowel disorders (post-infection IBS)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Diagnose post-infectious IBS on the basis of recurrent abdominal pain with altered bowel habit following an episode of infective gastroenteritis, after exclusion of alarm features.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP / NICE CG61 irritable bowel syndrome management
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Manage IBS in primary care with dietary measures, antispasmodics for cramping and low-dose tricyclic neuromodulation for visceral pain; escalate only if alarm features emerge.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Oral rehydration therapy principles for acute diarrhoeal fluid loss in adults
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Replace ongoing diarrhoeal losses with frequent small-volume oral rehydration solution; escalate to IV fluids if oral intake cannot be tolerated.
